ganization, but he used them for
the people. He nominated his man,
but that is all. 1t remained a con
sentative convention, the most
class conscious ever assembled in
this country. 1t will be the con-
sentative party of the future. But,
more potent than Office holders,
Roosevelt had a peoples conf
dence.


Bryan controlled this convention
and he has no Office holders. There
were no doubt Office seekers here,
but no organization. Bryan's nomi-
nation was dominated with his
strong personality by consent of
the many millions of more or less
confused radicals. Bryan has had
his own way here because his
friends trust him and his enemies
sneeringly pay out all the rope he
wants. They hope to hold the Or
ganization for the future. Bryan
has probably grown not so con-
sentative as wary, and has, of
course, a pardonable human de-
sire to win. Upon the whole if he
has not made tis convention as
radical as he could have made it
or as radical as his radical friends
wished him to make it, he has
made it more radical than the
president has his. All of LaFol
lette's planks scorned at Chicago,
are in the platform here. There
is a timid tariff plank. The party
clings to the old superstition that
you can stop trusts with a police
mans club, that is, block an
economic law by criminal statute.
The railroad planks, aside from
that on physical valuation which is
as good as showing tendency to
tax privilege, still pin the party
faith to regulation. Bryan knows
better than that, has already said
so, but he is not saying so here.


The anti-injunction plank is
probably satisf actory to the con-
sentative element and labor, that is
that part of laboI which has not
yet come into contact with eco
nomic consciousn SIS. The radical
wing of the labor group would en-
dorse this plank so far as it goes.
but asks much moro.


Upon the whole, this convention
may be said to have been pro-
gressive, even liberal. but it is not
radical, though to conservatives it
may be radical. Privilege will call
it dangerous.


The spectacle of the casting
away of Mr.. Guffey and his dele
gates did not fool anybody and did
not hurt anybody. 1t won't lose
votes for the ticket and probably
will make some. The Guffey dele,
gates had legal right to the seats.
The contestants had moral right.
1t was perfectly safe to recognize
the moral right in this instance be-
cause Pennsylvania is not a doubt
full state. The democratic machine
there would not help the real demo
Gratis carry it il it were doubtful
But the radicals are asking why
the same rule was not applied in
other contests exactly like this one.
These unsophisticated radicals
overlooked an important distinc-
ito. Other states are considered
doubtful.


The radicals in the republican
party will now depart from 1t and
the conservatives In the democratic
party will make one more effort to
beat the party this year and then
retire from it. There will be
radical group primarily represent
ing labor. which will not send a
to the old parties to ask
a plank in the platform. but will
